1: A revelation of God: 'At this my heart trembles and leaps from its place.' The physical response to theophanic approach is the universal human response to divine presence. Revelation 1:17 'when I saw him, I fell at his feet as though dead' — John's response to the glorified Christ. The trembling that Elihu describes before the approaching storm is what believers will experience before the returning Christ; the difference is that Christ says to the trembling: 'Do not be afraid' (Revelation 1:17).
2: A revelation of God: 'Listen carefully to the roar of his voice (bĕrōgez qōlô) and the rumble that goes out from his mouth.' John 12:28-29: when the Father speaks from heaven, the crowd says it thundered. Revelation 1:15 the glorified Christ's voice is 'like the sound of rushing waters'; Revelation 19:6 the sound of the great multitude is 'like the roar of rushing waters and like loud peals of thunder.' The divine voice that Elihu hears in the storm is the same voice John hears from the exalted Christ.
3: A shadow: 'He sends it under the whole sky, and his lightning to the ends of the earth.' The global reach of divine lightning prepares for Matthew 24:27: 'for as lightning that comes from the east is visible even in the west, so will be the coming of the Son of Man.' The lightning of creation-governance becomes the lightning of the Second Coming — the same Creator who governs through storms will return with theophanic power.
4: A revelation of God: 'He thunders in his majestic voice (bĕqôl gĕʾônô).' The voice of divine majesty. Revelation 1:15 'his voice was like the sound of rushing waters'; Revelation 19:1 'a great multitude in heaven shouting... salvation and glory and power belong to our God.' The majestic voice that Elihu hears in the storm is ultimately the voice of Christ — the Word whose voice is the instrument of creation (Hebrews 1:2 'through whom also he made the universe') and judgment.
5: A revelation of God: 'God thunders astoundingly with his voice; he does great things beyond our understanding (wĕlōʾ nēḏāʿ).' The incomprehensible works of God. 1 Corinthians 2:9 'no eye has seen, no ear has heard, no human mind has conceived what God has prepared for those who love him — but God has revealed it to us by his Spirit.' The wonders beyond understanding are revealed through Christ (Colossians 2:2-3) and accessible to those who have 'the mind of Christ' (1 Corinthians 2:16).
6: A revelation of God: 'For he says to the snow: Fall upon the earth.' The divine command to meteorological phenomena. Mark 4:39 Jesus 'rebuked the wind and said to the waves, Quiet! Be still! And the wind died down and it was completely calm.' The disciples ask: 'Who is this? Even the wind and the waves obey him!' — the answer is: the Creator who gave the commands Elihu now describes. Christ speaks to weather because he is the one who originally commanded weather into existence.
7: A revelation of God: 'He seals the hand of every person so that all humanity may perceive his work.' The divine governance that stops human activity to create attentional space for perceiving God. John 6:44 'no one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws them' — the Father's sealing of human activity is his drawing-work, creating the receptivity for recognizing the Son. Acts 2:2-4 the Pentecost wind (the same meteorological vocabulary) seals a new community for the perception of Christ's work.
8: The theme: 'Then the wild animals go into their dens and stay in their lairs.' The ordered response of creation to divine weather is evidence that creation 'knows its place.' The NT applies this creaturely knowledge to the disciples: John 10:27 'my sheep listen to my voice; I know them, and they follow me.' The animal that responds instinctively to the divine storm is the type of the believer who responds to Christ's voice.
9: A revelation of God: 'From the south comes the whirlwind (sûp̄āh).' The whirlwind that Elihu announces is the same form (sĕʿārāh) from which YHWH will answer in Job 38:1. Acts 2:2 'suddenly a sound like the blowing of a violent wind came from heaven and filled the whole house' — the Pentecost wind that announces the presence and work of the Holy Spirit whom Christ sends (John 15:26) is the NT theophanic wind.
10: A revelation of God: 'By the breath of God (mĕnišmaṯ-ʾēl) ice is formed.' The divine breath that creates. John 20:22 'he breathed on them and said, Receive the Holy Spirit' — the risen Christ breathes the Spirit into his disciples, the new-creation counterpart to the creation breath of Genesis 2:7. The breath that forms ice in winter forms new life in resurrection — both are the activity of the same Spirit.
11: A revelation of God: 'He loads the clouds with moisture and scatters his lightning-cloud.' The meteorological logistics as ongoing divine governance. Hebrews 1:3 'sustaining all things by his powerful word' — the Christ who sustains creation does so word by word, including the loading of clouds with precisely the moisture needed. Colossians 1:17 'in him all things hold together' means the weather held together in Christ.
12: A revelation of God: 'It turns in every direction by his guidance (bĕtaḥbûlōṯāyw).' The nautical steering-metaphor for divine providence. Ephesians 1:11 'having been predestined according to the plan of him who works out everything in conformity with the purpose of his will' — the same exhaustive navigational sovereignty that Elihu ascribes to storm-governance, Paul ascribes to the providential governance of salvation history in Christ.
13: A revelation of God: 'He brings it whether as discipline (lĕšēbeṭ), for his land's sake, or as steadfast love (lĕḥeseḏ).' The three-way purpose of divine storms — discipline, provision, and love — is applied by Paul to all things: Romans 8:28 'in all things God works for the good of those who love him.' The same providential logic that Elihu finds in weather, Paul finds in all circumstances for those in Christ.
14: A revelation of God: 'Listen to this, Job; stop and consider (hiṯbônān) the wondrous works of God.' The contemplative arrest — the call to stop and perceive. Psalms 46:10 'be still and know that I am God.' Christ issues the same invitation: Matthew 11:28-29 'come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me.' The 'stop and consider' becomes 'come and rest' in Christ.
15: The theme: 'Do you know how God arranged these things and caused the lightning in his cloud to flash?' The epistemic gap between creature and Creator. 1 Corinthians 13:12 'for now we see only a reflection as in a mirror; then we shall see face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I am fully known.' The partial knowledge that Elihu acknowledges will be resolved in the eschatological knowledge of seeing Christ face to face.
16: A revelation of God: 'Do you understand how the clouds are balanced — the wonders of the one who is perfect in knowledge (tĕmîm dēʿôṯ)?' The perfect knowledge is located in Christ: Colossians 2:3 'in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.' The perfect knowledge Elihu claims imperfectly is Christ's actual possession — hidden in him for those who seek.
17: A revelation of God: 'Why your clothes are warm when the earth lies still under the south wind?' The immediate bodily experience of divine care. Matthew 6:28-30 'see how the flowers of the field grow... if that is how God clothes the grass of the field, will he not much more clothe you?' — Jesus points to the same mundane meteorological-botanical evidence of divine care, drawing the same theological conclusion Elihu implies.
18: A revelation of God: 'Have you joined him in spreading out the sky (rāqîaʿ), firm and hard as a poured metal mirror?' The creation of the firmament. Proverbs 8:27 Wisdom was present 'when he set the heavens in place, when he marked out the horizon on the face of the deep.' Colossians 1:16 'all things in heaven and on earth... all things have been created through him and for him' — including the sky that Elihu describes Job as unable to have helped spread out.
19: The theme: 'Tell us what we should say to him — we cannot arrange our words because of our darkness.' Human darkness before God. The NT does not remove this darkness through better human speech but through divine provision: Romans 8:26 'the Spirit himself intercedes for us through wordless groans... the Spirit intercedes for God's people in accordance with the will of God.' Christ provides both the mediator (himself) and the helper (the Spirit) for the failure of human speech before God.
20: The theme: 'Should he be told that I want to speak? Can anyone speak to him without being consumed?' The impossibility of unmediated human speech to God. 1 Timothy 2:5 'there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus.' The consumption that Elihu fears — the creature consumed by divine presence — is what Christ prevents through his mediatorial work (Hebrews 9:14-15).
21: A revelation of God: 'People cannot look at the bright light in the clouds.' The divine inaccessibility. 1 Timothy 6:16 God 'lives in unapproachable light, whom no one has seen or can see.' The same inaccessible light is what John 1:14 says 'became flesh and made his dwelling among us' — the unapproachable light becomes approachable in Christ. 2 Corinthians 4:6 'God, who said, “Let light shine out of darkness,” made his light shine in our hearts to give us the light of the knowledge of God’s glory displayed in the face of Christ.'
22: A revelation of God: 'From the north comes golden brightness; God comes in fearsome majesty (nôrāʾ hôḏ).' The divine approach from the north in golden light. Revelation 4:3 'there was a rainbow that shone like an emerald encircling the throne'; Revelation 21:11 the new Jerusalem 'shone with the glory of God, and its brilliance was like that of a very precious jewel.' The fearsome majesty Elihu perceives in the approaching storm is the glory that surrounds the enthroned Christ.
23: A revelation of God: 'As for the Almighty (Šaddai), we cannot reach him; he is great in power (ḵōaḥ), in justice (mišpāṭ), and in abundant righteousness (ṣĕḏāqāh).' The triple attribute is exactly Paul's doxology in Romans 11:33: 'the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God; how unsearchable his judgments and his paths beyond tracing out!' Philippians 4:7 'the peace of God, which transcends all understanding.' The unreachable God is reached through Christ (Ephesians 2:13; Job 3:12).
24: A revelation of God: 'He does not attend to those who are wise in their own eyes.' God bypasses self-sufficient wisdom. 1 Corinthians 1:25-29 'God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise... not many of you were wise by human standards... but God chose the lowly things of this world and the despised things.' Christ's incarnation itself is the pattern: the Wisdom of God comes as a carpenter's son (Matthew 13:54-57), bypassing those wise in their own eyes.
